retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Preventive Measures
retrieved_docs.content: The utilization of prophylactic negative pressure therapy in post-surgical wounds has been proposed for specific high-risk surgical cases and contaminated wounds. [56] [57] Although data generally support this practice in high-risk surgeries, outcomes vary, likely due to differences in wound contamination levels and patient and wound characteristics. [58] [59] Data do not suggest that the selection of surgical dressings for closed incisions significantly affects the incidence of surgical site infections. [60] However, the use of prophylactic wound protectors, available for laparoscopy, laparotomy, and orthopedic incisions, appears to be beneficial. [61] [62] [63] [64] [65] [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Preventive Measures
retrieved_docs.content: Delayed primary closure, commonly utilized in cases of significant contamination, has historically been employed to mitigate surgical site infections in specific patient populations. However, a meta-analysis of randomized studies did not demonstrate any significant clinical benefit associated with this practice. [54] [55] Additionally, antibiotic selection tailored to the type of surgical procedure and the prevalent microbes encountered remains crucial in preventing surgical site infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Preventive Measures
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive measures should be taken to mitigate postoperative infections, with a checklist approach and attention to known risk factors being paramount. The "CDC and Health Infection Control Practice Advisory Committee Guideline for the Prevention of Surgical Site Infections," a comprehensive, evidence-based guideline published in 2017, is highly recommended for this purpose. These measures can be categorized into pre-procedural, perioperative, and intraoperative phases. Key pre-procedural considerations include optimizing chronic health issues such as glucose control, medication assessment, addressing chronic wounds/infections, and smoking cessation. Perioperative steps may entail preoperative showers, hair clipping, administering operation-specific antibiotics, and appropriate skin preparation. Additionally, maintaining optimal intraoperative conditions, including temperature, air circulation, and sterility, is imperative for preventing wound infections. [29] [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Preventive Measures
retrieved_docs.content: The routine use of surgical drains is discouraged due to uncertainty regarding their efficacy in preventing surgical site infections, and they may impede early patient mobilization. [51] [52] [53] If utilized, surgical drains should be promptly removed. Various measures have been taken to reduce the incidence of surgical site infections, including antibiotic irrigation, topical antimicrobial gels, antibiotic-impregnated suture material, and antiseptic dressings, among others. However, no definitive evidence currently exists demonstrating the significant efficacy of these interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical site infections represent the primary source of nosocomial infections in surgical patients. [1] Before the advent of the germ theory of infection and the recognition of the preventive efficacy of antisepsis, the incidence of postoperative surgical infections was alarmingly high, often resulting in limb amputation or mortality. However, the adoption of antiseptic techniques significantly ameliorated patient outcomes. [2] [3] [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify risk factors associated with postoperative wound infections during preoperative assessments. Implement evidence-based preventive measures to minimize the risk of postoperative wound infections. Apply appropriate wound care techniques to prevent and manage postoperative wound infections effectively. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare teams to coordinate follow-up care and interventions, thereby optimizing patient outcomes and preventing postoperative wound infections. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical site infections contribute significantly to postoperative morbidity and mortality rates, with current data revealing that they are responsible for over 2 million nosocomial infections in the United States. [8] [9] [10] [11] The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) classify surgical site infections into categories such as superficial, deep incisional, or organ/space infections. Any surgical wounds declared infected or opened by the surgeon are designated as surgical site infections. These infections must occur within 30 days following surgery or within 1 year after implantation to meet the classification criteria. For surgical site infection categories, please refer to the January 2023 CDC-National Healthcare Safety Network (NHSN) Patient Safety Component Manual for a more detailed description and information.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although the adoption of enhanced preventive measures has led to a decline in the incidence of surgical site infections over time, they still significantly impact morbidity and mortality. Surgical site infections contribute to 20% of all healthcare-associated infections. [27] [28] Patients who develop surgical site infections face higher chances of requiring admission to an intensive care unit (ICU) and are associated with mortality risks ranging from 2 to 11 times greater. Additionally, they are 5 times more likely to experience hospital readmission. Surgical site infections constitute the most common cause of unplanned hospital readmissions in the postoperative period. [29] [30] [31] [1] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: During the perioperative period, patients interact with numerous healthcare professionals who are directly or indirectly engaged in mitigating factors associated with the risk of postoperative wound infections. Preoperatively, it is imperative to identify and address modifiable risk factors and provide the patient with appropriate counseling regarding potential risks. Although discussions about risks are primarily conducted by the nursing staff, anesthesiologists, and surgeons, the interprofessional healthcare team members must educate patients and reinforce preventive measures.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Certain elective conditions can and should be optimized before surgical procedures. These factors include smoking cessation, weight loss, coagulation cascade normalization, glucose control optimization, and stabilization of other comorbidities. Procedure-related risk factors include:

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 0.5% to 3% of all surgical patients will develop a surgical site infection. [26] However, the growing prevalence of outpatient surgery poses challenges in gathering comprehensive postoperative data. To address this, the NHSN has recently initiated protocols to collect data on surgical site infections resulting from procedures conducted in ambulatory surgical centers. Notably, surgical site infections often manifest after visits to ambulatory surgical centers or hospital discharge, documented in outpatient notes that may not be integrated into the hospital record. Therefore, any data reported by the CDC and NHSN should be interpreted within this contextual framework.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tools and accessories, including stethoscopes, blood pressure cuffs, patient transfer slides, tourniquets, and computer keyboards, should be regularly cleaned to prevent bacterial contamination. [19] [20] [21] [22] [23] Surgical devices such as anesthesia or cautery units, suction machines, operating room lighting, and patient transfer aids can also serve as potential sources of contamination. Towels, sheets, and similar materials should be stored in closed cabinets or outside the operating room. Utilizing appropriate scrubbing techniques and double gloving can help reduce the incidence of postoperative infections. [24] The World Health Organization (WHO) surgical checklist aims to enhance communication, prevent complications, and improve safety and outcomes, including the prevention of surgical site infections. Surgical procedures are categorized as clean, clean-contaminated, contaminated, and dirty-infected, each associated with varying rates of postoperative wound infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Reducing patient skin flora through a preoperative chlorhexidine shower is commonly advised the night before and/or on the day of surgery. Hair removal is recommended, preferably using clippers, immediately before surgery. However, clippers are not recommended before scrotal surgery due to the potential for excessive skin trauma. In most procedures and specialties, chlorhexidine and alcohol-based agents are typically preferred and recommended for skin preparation.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Early recognition and prompt treatment of all surgical wounds are crucial for achieving the most favorable prognosis. However, prioritizing strict adherence to a prevention protocol represents the most prudent approach. Various models have been developed across different surgical specialties to aid in identifying high-risk patients and prevent surgical site infections. For instance, a study focusing on patients with colorectal cancer identified physiological factors, tumor characteristics, and type of surgery as reliable predictive factors for the development of surgical site infection. [87] Additionally, surgical factors such as the type of procedure, emergency surgery, wound class (dirty-infected), placement of surgical drains, surgeon experience, prolonged operating time, and certain postoperative factors like extended hospital stays and the need for intraoperative transfusions were identified as independent risk factors for surgical site infections. [88]

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Certain identifiable and modifiable risk factors in patients, such as body mass index, diabetic control, and smoking status, can help minimize postoperative wound infections. Elective surgeries often entail preoperative education and counseling. Before initiating certain procedures, patients may be encouraged to lose weight, adhere to a pharmacological regimen, and maintain healthy habits.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Wound Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Preventive Measures
retrieved_docs.content: A large study from Japan demonstrated a significant reduction in surgical site infections by including a perioperative oral cleaning regimen. This included removal of tartar, plaque, and scale, professional dental cleaning, optimal denture care, including adjustments, and high-quality general dental care, including extractions as needed before surgery. [49] In a large trial, the practice of changing surgical gloves and instruments immediately before abdominal wound closure resulted in a statistically significant decrease in the rate of surgical site infections. Although preliminary, these findings suggest the need for further studies to validate this approach. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2658
retrieved_docs.content: Incision and drainage of skin and subcutaneous tissue is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the subkutánní tkáň, the subcutaneous tissue layer beneath the skin, allowing removal of pus or fluid collections located in this tissue to promote healing and prevent infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Medicated dressings with antiinfectives are a specific category of medicated dressings, distinguished by the inclusion of antimicrobial agents that help prevent or treat infection in wounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2875
retrieved_docs.content: التهاب النسيج الخلوي (cellulitis) is clinically associated with قسطرة قلبية (cardiac catheterization). The invasive nature of cardiac catheterization can create a portal for bacterial entry at the vascular access site, increasing the risk of developing cellulitis in the surrounding skin and subcutaneous tissue. Recognizing this association emphasizes the importance of strict aseptic technique, careful site monitoring, and early intervention for infection in patients undergoing cardiac catheterization, thereby improving postoperative outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: of episiotomy, preventive incision). This integrated framework clarifies how each procedure is related, categorized, and applied within obstetric care, supporting comprehensive understanding and coordinated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN and High risk are both clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that an unidentified type of cesarean scar and a high‑risk patient status are relevant factors in the consideration, planning, and postoperative management of cesarean deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1798
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with several obstetric and perinatal conditions, including الْتِهابُ المَشيمُوا والسَّلَى (chorioamnionitis and sepsis), تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (post‑delivery residual findings), and gestation twin (twin gestation). In addition, a low transverse cesarean section is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر, indicating that this common surgical delivery technique may be linked to an increased risk of premature membrane rupture. Recognizing these associations helps clinicians anticipate potential complications, guide monitoring strategies, and tailor management plans for patients experiencing twin pregnancies, infections of the fetal membranes, or postoperative outcomes following low transverse cesarean delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 666
retrieved_docs.content: OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES includes Surgical incisions as a subcategory, indicating that the practice of making incisions is a fundamental component of operative and anesthetic care. Surgical incisions are clinically linked to several serious postoperative complications. Specifically, Obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene and Postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction are both associated with the presence of surgical incisions, reflecting the risk of herniation and bowel compromise after incision closure. In addition, Surgical incisions are associated with the Arabic‑described conditions فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (a strangulated incisional hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (an incarcerated abdominal hernia), further emphasizing that improper healing or technical issues with incisions can lead to hernias that become trapped or strangulated, requiring prompt medical attention. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate, monitor for, and manage incision‑related hernia complications to improve surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2722
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive incision is a specific form of episiotomy that is performed prophylactically during childbirth to reduce the likelihood of severe perineal trauma; thus, it is directly related to the broader practice of episiotomy as a surgical technique used to enlarge the vaginal opening and protect maternal tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2386
retrieved_docs.content: جرح قطع (cut wound) is classified as a specific type of جرح مفتوح (open wound), indicating that cut wounds are a subset of open wounds and share the characteristic of tissue exposure that requires appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2398
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrical trachelotomy is linked to a preventive incision, indicating that the procedure incorporates a preventive incision to mitigate potential complications. This relationship underscores the importance of incision planning in obstetrical trachelotomy as a strategy to reduce procedural risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 784
retrieved_docs.content: Complication is linked to multiple open wounds that have not yet resulted in additional problems, and it is also associated with open wounds of the extremities. This relationship underscores that even when open wounds—particularly numerous ones or those located on the limbs—have not produced further complications, they are still considered relevant to the broader concept of complication in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: these interventions within the overall framework of pregnancy‑related surgical care. Post‑partum obstetric operations are connected to immediate post‑delivery investigation and care, ensuring continuity from ante‑partum monitoring through delivery and post‑partum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 829
retrieved_docs.content: Complication is linked to open wounds of the extremities, which are described in Arabic as “جرح مفتوح.” While open wounds of the extremities can be associated with complications, the presence of multiple open wounds does not necessarily result in a complication, indicating that several open wounds may occur without leading to adverse outcomes. This relationship highlights that the mere existence of open wounds, even when numerous, does not automatically imply a complication, emphasizing the need for careful clinical assessment to determine when an open wound of the extremities truly represents a complication.
